summary: Gruber follows up on his initial negative reaction to Apple removing logo stickers from product boxes. After reader pushback, he acknowledges the stronger argument: the stickers are plastic, while Apple has systematically eliminated plastic from its packaging. He changes his mind, agreeing that making stickers available at retail stores is a good compromise. He also shares poll results from three platforms showing roughly 69% approval of the change.
key_points: The weight of one extra sheet isn't the compelling argument — it's that the stickers are plastic in otherwise all-paper/cardboard packaging
Apple has systematically eliminated almost all plastic from its packaging, making the stickers an inconsistent holdout
Making stickers available at Apple retail stores rather than including them in every box is a reasonable compromise
Poll results across Twitter/X, Mastodon, and Threads show ~69% approval, with Twitter/X slightly less favorable to the change
money_quotes: The better argument against the stickers is that they're plastic.
All the other in-box paperwork is actual paper, and the packaging itself — including the interior structure — is all cardboard.
Apple has eliminated almost all plastic from its packaging over the years, including the clear shrink-wrap.
So consider my mind changed: eliminating the stickers from the box, but making them available to those who want them at Apple retail stores, is a good compromise.
If those stickers are 0.1mm thick, a stack of 1 billion of them would be 100km high. But that's still just one sheet amongst many others that Apple includes in every box.
key_insight: The real case against Apple's in-box stickers isn't volume or weight but that they're plastic — an anomaly in Apple's otherwise all-paper packaging — and Gruber is willing to change his mind when presented with the better argument.
